journal-title-group.journal-title: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports

title-group.article-title: No additive effect of creatine, caffeine, and sodium bicarbonate on intense exercise performance in endurance‐trained individuals

aff.named-content: The August Krogh Section for Human Physiology, Department of Nutrition, Exercise and Sports

author-notes.corresp:
* Correspondence
Morten Hostrup, The August Krogh Section for Human Physiology, Department of Nutrition, Exercise and Sports, University of Copenhagen, August Krogh Building, 2nd Floor, Universitetsparken 13, DK‐2100 Copenhagen, Denmark. Email: mhostrup@nexs.ku.dk

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2024 The Authors. Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science In Sports published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Athletes commonly use creatine, caffeine, and sodium bicarbonate for performance enhancement. While their isolated effects are well‐described, less is known about their potential additive effects.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Following a baseline trial, we randomized 12 endurance‐trained males (age: 25 ± 5 years, VO2max: 56.7 ± 4.6 mL kg−1 min−1; mean ± SD) and 11 females (age: 25 ± 3 years, VO2max: 50.2 ± 3.4 mL kg−1 min−1) to 5 days of creatine monohydrate (0.3 g kg−1 per day) or placebo loading, followed by a daily maintenance dose (0.04 g kg−1) throughout the study. After the loading period, subjects completed four trials in randomized order where they ingested caffeine (3 mg kg−1), sodium bicarbonate (0.3 g kg−1), placebo, or both caffeine and sodium bicarbonate before a maximal voluntary contraction (MVC), 15‐s sprint, and 6‐min time trial.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Compared to placebo, mean power output during 15‐s sprint was higher following loading with creatine than placebo (+34 W, 95% CI: 10 to 58, p = 0.008), but with no additional effect of caffeine (+10 W, 95% CI: −7 to 24, p = 0.156) or sodium bicarbonate (+5 W, 95% CI: −4 to 13, p = 0.397). Mean power output during 6‐min time trial was higher with caffeine (+12 W, 95% CI: 5 to 18, p = 0.001) and caffeine + sodium bicarbonate (+8 W, 95% CI: 0 to 15, p = 0.038), whereas sodium bicarbonate (–1 W, 95% CI: −7 to 6, p = 0.851) and creatine (–6 W, 95% CI: −15 to 4, p = 0.250) had no effects.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: While creatine and caffeine can enhance sprint‐ and time trial performance, respectively, these effects do not seem additive. Therefore, supplementing with either creatine or caffeine appears sufficient to enhance sprint or short intense exercise performance.

p.mixed-citation: Moesgaard L , Jessen S , Christensen PM , Bangsbo J , Hostrup M . No additive effect of creatine, caffeine, and sodium bicarbonate on intense exercise performance in endurance‐trained individuals. Scand J Med Sci Sports. 2024;34:e14629. doi:10.1111/sms.14629 38646853 PMC12810453

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Given that small margins often decide placements during sport competitions, athletes and their coaches often seek ergogenic aids that can potentially improve performance. Consequently, survey data has shown that athletes competing in both individual and team sports have a median use of three different supplements. 1 Three of the most commonly used ergogenic substances for which positive findings on exercise performance have been reported at a meta‐analytical level include creatine monohydrate, 2 caffeine 3 and sodium bicarbonate. 4 However, while numerous studies demonstrate that each of these substances have a performance‐enhancing potential, their ergogenic or ergolytic effects are less clear when used in combination.

Creatine, caffeine, and sodium bicarbonate enhance performance through different mechanisms. Creatine increases phosphocreatine content in skeletal muscle, 5 caffeine antagonizes adenosine signaling, 6 and sodium bicarbonate buffers H+ ions, leading to an increased diffusional gradient for H+ from muscle to plasma. 7 As no links between these mechanisms have been established, it seems unlikely that competitive inhibition would occur with co‐ingestion. Therefore, it is plausible that all these ergogenic aids could simultaneously contribute to increased exercise performance. However, while an additive or synergistic performance enhancing effect of concurrent use of these supplements seems plausible, combined ingestion of sodium bicarbonate and caffeine has not been shown to be superior to caffeine alone in terms of improving either rowing 8 or cycling time trial performance. 9 Likewise, a study by Griffen et al. reported no additive effect of creatine and sodium bicarbonate on power output during repeated sprints. 10 In contrast, Barber et al. reported that combination of creatine and sodium bicarbonate enhanced power output during repeated sprints more than creatine alone. 11 In addition, while Lee et al. found that ingestion of caffeine following a period of creatine loading enhanced repeated sprint performance and time to exhaustion more than creatine loading alone, 12 , 13 caffeine has also been found to blunt the ergogenic effect of creatine on knee extension torque during repeated maximal isokinetic contractions. 14

Given that creatine, caffeine, and sodium bicarbonate seem to have differential effects depending on exercise intensity and duration, it is possible that discrepancies in the literature relate to between‐study differences in test batteries. While caffeine consistently has been demonstrated to enhance time trial performances and open‐end endurance outcomes, 15 uncertainties still remain with regards to its potential effects on maximal power output 16 and muscle strength. 17 Similarly, the performance enhancing effects of creatine on sprint performance are well‐described, 18 whereas conflicting results have been reported for its potential to enhance endurance. 19 , 20 For sodium bicarbonate, mixed findings have been reported for both sprint‐ 21 , 22 and endurance 23 , 24 performance. Therefore, it remains unclear whether there is an additive effect of creatine, caffeine, and sodium bicarbonate and whether this depends on how exercise performance is assessed. Such knowledge would have large implications for athletes competing in sports where less than 1% often separate medalists. 25 However, no studies have investigated the combined use of creatine, caffeine and sodium bicarbonate to maximize exercise performance.

Thus, we investigated the effect of combined ingestion of creatine monohydrate, caffeine and sodium bicarbonate on sprint and short intense exercise performance in endurance‐trained females and males. We hypothesized that caffeine and sodium bicarbonate would enhance short intense endurance exercise performance and would be additive, whereas caffeine and creatine would enhance sprint performance and would be additive.

sec.title: Subjects
sec.p: Thirty‐seven healthy endurance‐trained females and males volunteered to participate in the study. Inclusion criteria were between 18 and 45 years, endurance training >3 times per week during the 2 years leading up to the study, and VO2max of >50 and > 45 mL·kg−1·min−1 for males and females, 26 respectively. Exclusion criteria were smoking, chronic disease, use of creatine, prescription medication and blood donation within 3 months of commencement of the study. Twenty‐one subjects reported a habitual caffeine consumption between 1 and 5 cups of coffee per day. However, as habitual caffeine consumption has been reported not to affect the acute effect of caffeine on performance, subjects were not excluded based on habitual caffeine consumption. 27 Subjects were informed about risks and potential discomforts associated with the study procedures. Each subject provided their written and oral informed consent before inclusion in the study. The study was approved by the local ethics committee of Copenhagen, Denmark (H‐21035646), and all procedures were performed in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. All experimental procedures were performed at the Department of Nutrition, Exercise and Sports, University of Copenhagen. The study was registered in ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT05354999).

sec.title: Assessment of eligibility criteria
sec.p: Eligibility criteria were assessed during a screening where maximal oxygen consumption (VO2max) was assessed by indirect calorimetry during a ramp test to exhaustion on a bicycle ergometer (Monark LC7, Monark Exercise AB, Vansbro, Sweden). Following a 10 min warm‐up at 150 W or 100 W for males and females, respectively, the ramp test started and increased by 1 W every 2 s until exhaustion. Pulmonary gas exchange was measured during the test breath‐by‐breath with a gas analyzing system (Oxycon Pro, Viasys Healthcare, Hoechberg, Germany). Subjects were instructed to keep a cadence of approximately 90 rpm. Time of exhaustion was defined as the point where cadence fell below 75 rpm, despite strong verbal encouragement. VO2max was defined as the highest mean value recorded in any consecutive 30 s period before cessation of exercise. Incremental peak power output (iPPO) was defined as the power output reached at exhaustion. Subjects who fulfilled all inclusion criteria and none of the exclusion criteria were familiarized to the study procedures. This was done by completing a 6‐min time trial, a 15‐s sprint test, and maximal voluntary isometric contraction (MVC) of m. quadriceps femoris.

sec.title: Study design
sec.p: The study was designed as a double‐blinded, randomized, placebo‐controlled trial. Following a baseline trial, subjects were randomized to a 5‐day loading period of 0.3 g·kg bw−1 intake of creatine monohydrate 28 or placebo (glucose monohydrate) split into four equal doses throughout the day. This was followed by a daily maintenance dose of 0.04 g·kg−1 throughout the study. Creatine monohydrate and placebo were administered in identical capsules of 0.5 g. Compliance was ensured by daily contact with subjects. After the loading period, subjects attended four experimental trials in a randomized order‐balanced manner, with >96 h between trials. The trials differed only in whether subjects were administered caffeine (3 mg·kg−1), 15 sodium bicarbonate (0.3 g·kg−1), 7 the combination of caffeine and sodium bicarbonate, or placebo. The baseline trial only differed from the experimental trial in that a DXA‐scan was conducted, no supplements were administered, and no blood was sampled. An overview of the study design is presented in Figure 1.

caption.p: Graphical overview of the study design (A) and experimental trials (B). PLA, placebo; CAF, caffeine; SB, sodium bicarbonate; CAF + SB, combination of caffeine and sodium bicarbonate; DXA, dual‐energy x‐ray absorptiometry; VO2max, maximal oxygen uptake; iPPO, incremental peak power output. MVC, maximal voluntary contraction.

sec.title: Experimental trials
sec.p: At each experimental trial subjects were administered a beverage consisting of sugar free flavored water containing either caffeine, sodium bicarbonate, placebo, or both caffeine and sodium bicarbonate. Sodium chloride (0.05 g·kg−1) was dissolved in all beverages that did not contain sodium bicarbonate, to match the taste between beverages. 29 Subjects then rested for 60 min to allow caffeine and sodium bicarbonate to reach systemic circulation, 30 , 31 and were instructed to report side effects that occurred throughout the experimental trial. Thereafter, subjects performed a standardized warm‐up on a bicycle ergometer (Monark LC7, Monark Exercise AB, Vansbro, Sweden) consisting of 3 × 4 min at 30%, 40%, and 50% of iPPO, determined from the ramp test at the screening visit. After the warm‐up, subjects performed a 15‐s maximal sprint on a bicycle ergometer (Monark 839E; Monark Exercise AB, Vansbro, Sweden), followed by a 20 min cooldown at 30% iPPO (Monark LC7, Monark Exercise AB, Vansbro, Sweden). After 10 min of rest, subjects' MVC of m. quadriceps femoris was assessed. Then, subjects started a 6‐min time trial, in which participants were instructed to exert themselves maximally to produce as high mean power output as possible. All bicycling was performed with cleated shoes. Immediately after the 6‐min time trial subjects' MVC of m. quadriceps femoris was assessed again. Blood samples were also obtained before and 3 min after the 6‐min time trial and immediately analyzed for bicarbonate (HCO3 −) and pH using a blood gas analyzer (ABL 800 FLEX, Radiometer, Copenhagen, Denmark).

All trials were conducted at the same time of the day for each subject to avoid influence of circadian rhythm. Subjects were instructed to record and replicate their dietary intake on experimental days and to abstain from products with caffeine (i.e., coffee and energy drinks) and not consume any sodium bicarbonate products in the 24 h leading up to the experimental trials.

sec.title: 15‐s sprint
sec.p: The 15‐s sprint was performed on a bicycle ergometer (Monark 839E, Monark Exercise AB, Vansbro, Sweden) where power output was recorded using Monark software (Monark Anaerobic Test Software, v. 3.3). Subjects were instructed to pedal as fast as possible against a low resistance which increased to 9% or 7% of bodyweight for males and females, respectively, when subjects reached a cadence of 100 rpm (~1 s after initiating the sprint). At this point, recording of mean and peak power output was initiated for 15 s.

sec.title: 6‐min time trial
sec.p: The 6‐min time trial was performed on a bicycle ergometer (Monark LC7, Monark Exercise AB, Vansbro, Sweden) connected to a computer where cadence and power output were recorded (Monark Test Software, v. 1.1.9). Before the test was initiated, subjects pedaled at a cadence of 80–100 rpm at 150 W for 1 min and were instructed to maintain this cadence throughout the time trial. During the time trial subjects were able to adjust the load (W) (up or down) manually, while being blinded to the absolute load and rpm.

sec.title: Maximal voluntary isometric contraction
sec.p: For measurement of MVC of m. quadriceps femoris subjects were seated on a table in an upright position, with their thighs parallel to the floor and their right leg positioned with a knee joint angle of 90° flexion. Subjects used their arms to hold on to metal bars secured to the table. Isometric contraction force was recorded using a strain gauge (Tedea‐Huntleigh, UK) strapped around the right ankle superior to the malleoli. The strain gauge signal was fed to an amplifier that was connected to a computer. Data were recorded at 1 kHz in LabChart 7 (ADInstruments, CA, USA). Subjects performed four MVCs of 3–4 s, interspersed by 1 min of recovery.

sec.title: Dual‐energy x‐ray absorptiometry
sec.p: At the baseline trial, two consecutive DXA scans were performed to account for scan‐to‐scan variation. 32 Scans were performed at medium speeds according to the manufacturer's guidelines. Subjects were placed in the scanner undressed and euhydrated in a standardized supine position. The scanner was calibrated before scans using daily calibration procedures (Lunar “System Quality Assurance”).

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: Statistical analysis was performed in SPSS version 27 (IBM Corp., Armonk, NY). Data were tested for normality using the Shapiro–Wilk test and Q–Q plots. Data were normally distributed. Data are presented as means ± SD unless otherwise specified, along with exact p‐values (unless <0.001) to represent probability. To assess differences between groups and treatments, two‐tailed linear mixed modeling was used with group and treatment as fixed factors, and subjects as a random factor. The effects of creatine loading were assessed as between‐group differences between creatine loading and placebo loading, whereas the effects of caffeine and sodium bicarbonate were assessed as within‐subject differences between trials. Sample size was determined based on the effect size from a previous study, where a 7 W difference in 6‐min rowing performance was sufficient to find a statistical difference between caffeine and placebo with 12 participants. 8

sec.title: Subjects and compliance
sec.p: Of 37 subjects initially screened, 11 did not meet the eligibility criteria, and two withdrew their consent before commencing the study. One subject from the creatine group withdrew due to personal reasons during the study. Twenty‐three subjects (12 males and 11 females) completed the study (Figure 2). Characteristics of the subjects that completed the study are presented in Table 1. Compliance during the loading period was 100% for all subjects. Two subjects from the creatine group missed a single daily maintenance dose during the study.

fn.p: Abbreviations: iPPO, incremental peak power output during incremental test, VO2max, maximal oxygen uptake.

sec.title: Side effects
sec.p: No subjects reported side effects during creatine or placebo loading. Eight subjects reported side effects during the sodium bicarbonate trial, 12 subjects reported side effects during the caffeine + sodium bicarbonate trial, one subject reported side effects during the caffeine trial, and two subjects reported side effects during the placebo trial. Side effects included stomach discomfort (n = 11), nausea (n = 8), diarrhea (n = 9), and dizziness (n = 2), but not to a degree in which tests could not be completed.

sec.title: 15‐s sprint
sec.p: At baseline, peak and mean power during the 15‐s sprint was 1042 ± 305 W and 772 ± 258 W for the creatine loading group and 911 ± 199 W and 685 ± 159 W for the placebo loading group, respectively, with no differences between groups (peak power: p = 0.229; mean power: p = 0.336).

Mean power output during the 15‐s sprint was greater in the creatine group than the placebo group in all experimental trials (main effect: +34 W, 95% CI: 10 to 58, p = 0.008; Figure 3A), with no difference between treatments (group × treatment interaction: p = 0.518; Figure 3A). Mean power output was non‐significantly higher with caffeine (+8 W, 95% CI: −1 to 18, p = 0.092) and caffeine + sodium bicarbonate (+7 W, 95% CI: −1 to 14, p = 0.070) than placebo (Figure 3A). There was no effect of sodium bicarbonate on mean power output compared to placebo (−1 W, 95% CI: −10 to 8, p = 0.800; Figure 3A).

Peak power output during the 15‐s sprint was higher with caffeine than placebo (+18 W, 95% CI: 2 to 35, p = 0.031; Figure 3B), with a non‐significantly greater peak power output in the creatine group than the placebo group (group × treatment interaction: +40 W, 95% CI: −8 to 88, p = 0.094; Figure 3B). However, there was no effect of creatine (+30 W, 95% CI: −5 to 65, p = 0.210; Figure 3B), sodium bicarbonate (+1 W, 95% CI: −20 to 22, p = 0.921; Figure 3B), and caffeine + sodium bicarbonate (+5 W, 95% CI: −12 to 22, p = 0.566; Figure 3B) on peak power output compared to placebo.

caption.p: Individual and mean changes in mean (A) and peak power output (B) during 15‐s sprint from baseline to treatment with placebo (PLA), caffeine (CAF; 3 mg·kg−1), sodium bicarbonate (SB; 0.3 g·kg−1), and both caffeine and sodium bicarbonate (CAF + SB). Gray circles indicate subjects who underwent a 5‐day loading period with creatine monohydrate (n = 11). White circles indicate subjects who underwent a 5‐day loading period with placebo (n = 12). ##Different from placebo loading (p < 0.01). $Main effect of caffeine compared to placebo (p < 0.05).

sec.title: 6‐min time trial
sec.p: At baseline, mean power during the 6‐min time trial was 295 ± 68 W for the creatine group and 278 ± 54 W for the placebo group, with no differences between groups (p = 0.533).

Mean power output during the 6‐min time trial was non‐significantly greater with caffeine in the placebo group than the creatine group (group × treatment interaction: +11 W, 95% CI: −1 to 23, p = 0.075; Figure 4), whereas no group difference was observed with caffeine + sodium bicarbonate (group × treatment interaction: 1 W, 95% CI: −13 to 15, p = 0.899; Figure 4). Mean power output was higher with caffeine (+12 W, 95% CI: 5 to 18, p = 0.001) and caffeine + sodium bicarbonate (+8 W, 95% CI: 0 to 15, p = 0.038) than placebo (Figure 4), with no difference between caffeine and caffeine + sodium bicarbonate (4 W, 95% CI: −1 to 9, p = 0.132; Figure 4). There was no effect of creatine (−6 W, 95% CI: −16 to 4, p = 0.250; Figure 4) and sodium bicarbonate (−1 W, 95% CI: −7 to 6, p = 0.851; Figure 4) on mean power output compared to the placebo loading group and placebo trial, respectively.

caption.p: Individual and mean changes in mean power output during 6‐min time trial from baseline to treatment with placebo (PLA), caffeine (CAF; 3 mg·kg−1), sodium bicarbonate (SB; 0.3 g·kg−1), and both caffeine and sodium bicarbonate (CAF + SB). Gray circles indicate subjects who underwent a 5‐day loading period with creatine monohydrate (n = 11). White circles indicate subjects who underwent a 5‐day loading period with placebo (n = 12). *Different from PLA (p < 0.05).

sec.title: Maximal voluntary isometric contraction
sec.p: At baseline, MVC was 567 ± 124 N and 528 ± 129 N before the 6‐min time trial and 487 ± 83 N and 477 ± 97 after the 6‐min time trial in the creatine loading group and placebo loading group, respectively, with no differences between groups (before 6‐min time trial: p = 0.463; after 6‐min time trial: p = 0.791).

Before the 6‐min time trial MVC was higher in the creatine group than the placebo group only during the placebo trial (+33 N, 95% CI: 2 to 65, p = 0.039, Figure 5A), but with no difference between trials (group × treatment interaction: p = 0.304; Figure 5A). A higher MVC was observed with caffeine (+24 N, 95% CI: 11 to 37, p = 0.001; Figure 5A) and caffeine + sodium bicarbonate (+21 N, 95% CI: 7 to 34, p = 0.004; Figure 5A) compared to sodium bicarbonate, but not compared to placebo (caffeine: +9 N, 95% CI: −2 to 21, p = 0.108; caffeine + sodium bicarbonate: +6 N, 95% CI: −13 to 25, p = 0.527; Figure 5A). Correspondingly, a non‐significantly lower MVC was observed in sodium bicarbonate than placebo (−14 N, 95% CI: −30 to 1, p = 0.071, Figure 5A). None of the treatments had any apparent effects on MVC following the 6‐min time trial (p = 0.836; Figure 5B).

caption.p: Individual and mean changes in maximal voluntary isometric contraction (MVC) from baseline to treatment with placebo (PLA), caffeine (CAF; 3 mg·kg−1), sodium bicarbonate (SB; 0.3 g·kg−1), and both caffeine and sodium bicarbonate (CAF + SB). (A) Before 6‐min time trial. (B) After 6‐min time trial. Gray circles indicate subjects who underwent a 5‐day loading period with creatine monohydrate (n = 11). White circles indicate subjects who underwent a 5‐day loading period with placebo (n = 12). #Between‐group difference (p < 0.05). §Different from SB (Main effect: p < 0.05).

sec.title: Blood bicarbonate and pH
sec.p: Resting blood bicarbonate (HCO3 −) and pH was higher with sodium bicarbonate (HCO3 −: +5.6 mmol/L, 95% CI: 4.7 to 6.5, p < 0.001; pH: +0.08, 95% CI: 0.06 to 0.09, p < 0.001; Figure 6) and caffeine + sodium bicarbonate (HCO3 −: +5.3 mmol/L, 95% CI: 4.8 to 5.8, p < 0.001; pH: +0.08, 95% CI: 0.07 to 0.09, p < 0.001; Figure 6) than placebo and remained higher in both sodium bicarbonate (HCO3 −: +3.4 mmol/L, 95% CI: 2.5 to 4.3, p < 0.001; pH: +0.08, 95% CI: 0.06 to 0.10, p < 0.001; Figure 6) and caffeine + sodium bicarbonate (HCO3 −: +1.8 mmol/L, 95% CI: 1.1 to 2.5, p < 0.001; pH: +0.05, 95% CI: 0.03 to 0.07, p < 0.001; Figure 6) following the 6‐min time trial, whereas HCO3 − (−0.9 mmol/L, 95% CI: −1.8 to −0.1, p = 0.028; Figure 6B) and pH (−0.03, 95% CI: −0.06 to 0.0, p = 0.026; Figure 6D) were lower with caffeine than placebo. In addition, HCO3 − before the performance tests was not correlated with mean power output during either 6‐min time trial (r = −0.072, p = 0.505) or 15‐s sprint (r = 0.007, p = 0.952).

caption.p: Individual and mean blood bicarbonate (HCO3 −) and pH before and after a 6‐min time trial with either placebo (PLA), caffeine (CAF; 3 mg·kg−1), sodium bicarbonate (SB; 0.3 g·kg−1), or both caffeine and sodium bicarbonate (CAF + SB). (A): HCO3 −before 6‐min time trial. (B) HCO3 − after 6‐min time trial. (C) pH before 6‐min time trial. (D) pH after 6‐min time trial. Gray circles indicate subjects who underwent a 5‐day loading period with creatine monohydrate (n = 11). White circles indicate subjects who underwent a 5‐day loading period with placebo (n = 12). *Different from PLA (p < 0.05). **Different from PLA (p < 0.01).

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: The main finding of the present study was that while creatine monohydrate and caffeine respectively enhanced 15‐s sprint and 6‐min time trial performance, there was no additive, synergistic, or attenuated effect of co‐ingesting caffeine and creatine monohydrate on exercise performance. Sodium bicarbonate alone or in combination with creatine monohydrate and caffeine did not affect any measures of performance.

While creatine, caffeine, and sodium bicarbonate are among the most widely used substances by athletes and all have potential to enhance measures of exercise performance when ingested individually, 5 , 7 , 15 their combined effects are unclear. We found no additive effects on isometric muscle strength, sprint ability or time trial performance of short duration. Consistent with the well‐known effects of creatine and caffeine, we found them individually to enhance 15‐s sprint performance and 6‐min time trial performance, respectively. This coincides with studies showing that creatine predominantly improves sprint performance lasting <30 s, 33 whereas caffeine primarily improves performance during longer duration exercise. 15 Unlike the present study, Lee et al. found that ingestion of caffeine following a period of creatine loading resulted in greater mean power output during 10‐s bicycle sprints than creatine loading followed by placebo ingestion, suggesting that co‐ingestion of caffeine and creatine may improve sprint performance more than creatine alone. 12 Whereas subjects in the study by Lee et al. were instructed to abstain from caffeine during the entire creatine loading period, this was only the case in the 24 h leading up to the experimental trials in the present study. Although habitual caffeine consumption may have affected the results, 14 , 34 , 35 this seems unlikely given that creatine loading improved sprint performance in both the study by Lee et al. and the present study. Instead, the discrepancy in results may be explained by differences in training status. As the subjects in the present study were endurance‐trained, it can be speculated that the effect of caffeine on sprint performance may be more profound in individuals of lower training status. Despite this, our finding that neither the effect of creatine nor caffeine is attenuated by co‐ingestion suggests that supplementing with both creatine and caffeine could be beneficial for athletes in sports that involve both sprints and longer duration high‐intensity exercise, such as soccer and basketball, but also various cycling disciplines where both the ability to sprint and sustain a high power output can decide the outcome of a race.

While sodium bicarbonate effectively increased blood HCO3 − and pH, it did not affect mean power output during the 6‐min time trial compared to placebo. It has been suggested that sodium bicarbonate‐induced changes in blood HCO3 − and pH of a sufficient magnitude can drive an increase in exercise performance. 7 A recent meta‐analysis reported a positive effect on performance when blood HCO3 − increased by more than 4 mmol/L, with no further increase in performance with an increase above 6 mmol/L. 4 However, given that we observed an increase in HCO3 − of 5.6 mmol/L in the present study, this suggests that the lack of response observed with sodium bicarbonate was not due to not reaching a sufficient increase in blood HCO3 −. It also seems unlikely that the lack of performance enhancement with sodium bicarbonate was due to side effects 36 as the effects of caffeine on performance were not attenuated by co‐ingestion with sodium bicarbonate, despite subjects experiencing side effects, such as stomach discomfort, nausea, and diarrhea. Previous regression analysis has shown that sodium bicarbonate may primarily have an effect on shorter duration exercise tests (e.g., 1 min). 25 Therefore, this may explain the lack of performance increase during sodium bicarbonate trials. However, when a positive effect of sodium bicarbonate is observed this effect does not seem to be additive to the effect of caffeine on performance, 9 which supports the findings of the present study. As such, supplementation with sodium bicarbonate seems redundant for athletes seeking improvements in exercise performance of similar durations as the present study, and may even be detrimental due to a high number of individuals reporting gastrointestinal discomfort following ingestion of sodium bicarbonate, 37 although this did not seem to influence performance in the present study.

We also observed that whereas creatine increased MVC during the placebo trial, sodium bicarbonate tended to decrease MVC. As creatine supplementation has also been shown to increase both isometric 38 and dynamic 39 torque this suggests that creatine may be beneficial in sports that require single bouts of maximal force. In contrast, our results suggest that sodium bicarbonate should be avoided in such sports due to potentially attenuating maximal force production. Nevertheless, following the 6‐min time trial all effects on MVC had dissipated, suggesting that the effects of creatine and sodium bicarbonate on force production may not apply following exhaustive exercise. A study by Ansdell and Dekerle demonstrated that the decline in MVC following exhaustive exercise was lower with sodium bicarbonate than placebo. 40 This may explain why no differences between treatments were seen following the 6‐min time trial. However, based on the present findings, the applicability of a potential lower decline in MVC following sodium bicarbonate ingestion may be limited as the lower decline seems to be at the expense of absolute force production.

In summary, creatine improved sprint performance and MVC, whereas caffeine improved 6‐min time trial performance. Sodium bicarbonate did not affect any measures of performance. Co‐ingestion of creatine and caffeine did not increase exercise performance more than caffeine or creatine ingested alone. Therefore, it appears sufficient to supplement with creatine for sprint and maximal force production and caffeine for intense endurance performance. However, athletes in sports that involve both sprints and longer duration high‐intensity exercise may benefit from supplementation with both creatine and caffeine.

sec.title: Perspective
sec.p: While permitted ergogenic substances such as creatine, caffeine, and sodium bicarbonate only play a minor role in determining exercise performance, a potential effect of these substances on performance could be the difference between finishing first or second in highly competitive sports. 25 Therefore, if the ergogenic effects of these substances were additive this would be crucial information for high level competitive athletes. Likewise, if one of these substances blunt the effect of the other substances this could potentially be of great detriment to the athlete. Herein, we demonstrate that combined ingestion of creatine, caffeine, and sodium bicarbonate does not improve exercise performance more than creatine or caffeine ingested alone. Importantly however, co‐ingestion also did not attenuate the performance enhancing effect of creatine and caffeine, respectively. Thus, it is possible that athletes in sports that involve both sprints and sustained high‐intensity efforts could benefit from supplementation with both creatine and caffeine. In contrast, it is likely that ingesting only creatine or caffeine is sufficient to maximize performance for sprinters and endurance athletes, respectively.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ack.title: FUNDING INFORMATION
ack.p: The study was supported as part of the Novo Nordisk Foundation grant to Team Denmark to the research network “Training strategies and competition preparation.”

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: The dataset generated during the current study are not publicly available, but are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ref.mixed-citation: Baltazar‐Martins G , Brito de Souza D , Aguilar‐Navarro M , Muñoz‐Guerra J , Plata MDM , Del Coso J . Prevalence and patterns of dietary supplement use in elite Spanish athletes. J Int Soc Sports Nutr. 2019;16(1):30.31319850 10.1186/s12970-019-0296-5PMC6639916
